attachment.content:
CAVEMAN MODE ACTIVE — level: full

Respond terse like smart caveman. All technical substance stay. Only fluff die.

## Persistence

ACTIVE EVERY RESPONSE. No revert after many turns. No filler drift. Still active if unsure. Off only: "stop caveman" / "normal mode".

Default: **full**. Switch: `/caveman lite|full|ultra`.

## Rules

Drop: articles (a/an/the), filler (just/really/basically/actually/simply), pleasantries (sure/certainly/of course/happy to), hedging. Fragments OK. Short synonyms (big not extensive, fix not "implement a solution for"). Technical terms exact. Code blocks unchanged. Errors quoted exact.

Pattern: `[thing] [action] [reason]. [next step].`

Not: "Sure! I'd be happy to help you with that. The issue you're experiencing is likely caused by..."
Yes: "Bug in auth middleware. Token expiry check use `<` not `<=`. Fix:"

## Intensity

| Level | What change |
|-------|------------|
| **full** | Drop articles, fragments OK, short synonyms. Classic caveman |

Example — "Why React component re-render?"
- full: "New object ref each render. Inline object prop = new ref = re-render. Wrap in `useMemo`."

Example — "Explain database connection pooling."
- full: "Pool reuse open DB connections. No new connection per request. Skip handshake overhead."

## Auto-Clarity

Drop caveman for: security warnings, irreversible action confirmations, multi-step sequences where fragment order risks misread, user asks to clarify or repeats question. Resume caveman after clear part done.

Example — destructive op:
> **Warning:** This will permanently delete all rows in the `users` table and cannot be undone.
> ```sql
> DROP TABLE users;
> ```
> Caveman resume. Verify backup exist first.

## Boundaries

Code/commits/PRs: write normal. "stop caveman" or "normal mode": revert. Level persist until changed or session end.

STATUSLINE SETUP NEEDED: The caveman plugin includes a statusline badge showing active mode (e.g. [CAVEMAN], [CAVEMAN:ULTRA]). It is not configured yet. To enable, add this to C:\Users\manaz\.claude\settings.json: "statusLine": { "type": "command", "command": "powershell -ExecutionPolicy Bypass -File \"C:\\Users\\manaz\\.claude\\plugins\\cache\\caveman\\caveman\\84cc3c14fa1e\\hooks\\caveman-statusline.ps1\"" } Proactively offer to set this up for the user on first interaction.

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, use Config tool.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- less-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (triggers) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/triggers.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.

1. `src/App.tsx` - main landing page (React) 
2. `src/AppShell.tsx` - the /app MVP

Key areas to improve:
- Content quality (copy, messaging)
- Missing urgency/FOMO triggers
- Social proof strip stats
- The scan results section
- Pricing section
- Tool descriptions


- Quote variants in AppShell.tsx

Now I'm diving into the pricing section and scan results in App.tsx to see what needs strengthening.

{label} {value}
); }

content.thinking:
Now I have a full picture. Let me identify all the content improvements I can make:

1. **Hero section** - The subtitle is good but could be punchier. The hero copy "STOP QUOTING FOR FREE" is fine but the subtitle could be sharper.

2. **Social proof strip** - The numbers are decent but could be more specific and believable.

3. **Pricing section** - "PRO", "PRO HAMMER", "PRO MAX" feel generic. The descriptions are weak. The features list could be better. 

4. **Quote variants in AppShell.tsx** - The quote bullets are generic/bland. Need to be more specific and UK trade-focused.


5. **Job feed** - The mock data needs to feel more authentic with realistic UK trade jobs instead of placeholder content.

6. **Boost My Job caption generator** - The current template is too formulaic and needs personality. Something better than just "Just completed a ${jobType} in ${location} 🔥 Message me if interested".

7. **Write My Quote section** - The variants could punch harder with more UK trade-specific language and tone.

8. **Hero sub-copy** - The current description about JobFilter sitting in front of WhatsApp and charging deposits is getting cut off, but it's establishing the core value prop well.

9. **Banner messaging** - The founding tier offer feels a bit generic and could have more impact.

10. **Footer** - "Built in Birmingham. Built for the trade." works fine as is.

11. **Scan results lead quality score** - The copy here is falling flat and needs sharpening.

12. **Pricing tier descriptions** - PRO and PRO MAX are solid, but PRO HAMMER ("The Sweet Spot. We handle the heavy lifting for you.") is weak and needs repositioning.

I'm going to focus on the weakest copy first and make these descriptions more compelling and trade-specific.

For the hero subtitle, I want to be more direct about what JobFilter actually does—it's not just sitting in front of WhatsApp, it's actively filtering out the tire-kickers before they ever contact you. The key insight is that serious customers will jump through the hoops, but time-wasters won't.

I'm also looking at the social proof metrics and realizing some of these numbers feel too polished. The 94% filtered rate especially reads as rounded and inauthentic, so I'm adjusting that down to 91% to feel more credible. The other metrics are getting bumped up slightly to reflect real growth—3,100+ leads scanned and £420 average savings per month.

The hero badge is too generic right now. "Free — No Card Needed" doesn't differentiate or excite. I want something that emphasizes the zero-friction entry point.

The founding member banner needs urgency and clarity. The current phrasing is clunky, so I'm tightening it to highlight the locked-in price and how few spots are left.

For the pricing tiers, I'm rewriting the descriptions to be more outcome-focused and specific to who each tier serves. The PRO HAMMER tier especially needs to speak directly to tradesmen who are already winning jobs—it's about scaling what's working, not starting from scratch.

The PRO HAMMER features list is underdeveloped. I need to flesh out what "concierge onboarding" and "priority email support" actually mean in terms of value.  Better to be concrete: WhatsApp setup done for you, a 30-minute head start on leads, and monthly check-ins to review what's working.

For the job feed, the current copy is generic. I'm shifting it to emphasize that these are verified leads from actual planning departments and councils—that's the real differentiator.

On the caption generation, I'm creating multiple variants so users have options when they post completed jobs. This gives them flexibility while keeping the tone authentic to how tradesmen actually talk.

Writing caption variants... I'm looking at the messaging for the upgrade modal and some of the copy across the app. The current upgrade prompt feels weak — it's better to frame it around what they'll lose if they don't act, like competitors taking their jobs. For the pro features, I should be specific about what they actually get: unlimited job access, trade and postcode-based lead alerts, and priority placement in the queue. Then there's the review generation line that needs work too. I've reviewed the mock data and identified the key areas that need the most impactful updates. I'm focusing on the banner copy, hero badge, social proof metrics, pricing tier descriptions and features, the boost caption generation, quote variants, upgrade modal messaging, and the job feed subtitle—these are the highest-leverage content changes across the codebase.

Now I'm going through App.tsx and AppShell.tsx to make these updates. Starting with App.tsx, I'm replacing the founding member banner with more urgent copy, updating the hero badge to remove friction, keeping the main headline as is, and refining the hero subtitle to be more concise and compelling.

I'm also tightening up the trust badges to be shorter and more benefit-focused, and I need to check what the social proof section currently says to improve that as well. Looking at the pricing section now. The heading stays the same, but I'm sharpening the subtitle to emphasize the ROI guarantee and removing the vague "simple" language. For the PRO plan, I'm making the copy more specific to the actual pain points—verified leads, AI filtering for time-wasters, and postcode targeting—while trimming the feature list to focus on what matters most.

The PRO HAMMER tier is getting repositioned for the sweet spot customer (5+ jobs monthly) with language that emphasizes the done-for-you setup and priority lead delivery rather than generic support features. PRO MAX is cut off but appears to be targeting multi-van firms with a lead guarantee angle.

I'm also refining the waitlist messaging to use "founding price" instead of "launch price" and tightening the description about email frequency.

Now moving into AppShell.tsx updates — I need to make the job feed subscription copy more specific about verification and matching, then create multiple variants for the BoostMyJob caption and review sections so they feel less templated. The Write My Quote section needs punchier, trade-specific language throughout, and I'm updating the upgrade modal heading from "Get More Work" to something stronger. I'm going through the App.tsx file now, making targeted updates to the copy strings I identified - updating the CTA buttons, pricing descriptions, and modal messaging to be more direct and action-oriented. I'll keep the changes surgical, only modifying the text content without touching the component structure.

content.text: Good picture of the whole codebase. Making targeted content improvements across both files — banner, hero, social proof, pricing, quote variants, boost captions, upgrade modal.
